Spring is prime shedding season for cats, as longer days trigger a hormonal response that releases the heavy winter undercoat.
Cats shed their coats twice a year in response to changes in daylight (photoperiod), not temperature, and spring and autumn are the peak shedding times.
Indoor cats tend to shed more consistently year-round because artificial lighting disrupts the natural photoperiod cycle.
Regular brushing in spring can remove up to 90% of loose fur before it ends up on furniture or forms dangerous hairballs.
Spring is the main mating season for cats, and unspayed females can enter heat cycles every 2 to 3 weeks from spring through autumn.
A cat in heat may yowl loudly, roll on the floor, and become extremely affectionate, all behaviors designed to attract mates.
Female cats are induced ovulators, meaning they only release an egg in response to the stimulation of mating, not on a fixed monthly cycle.
Spring kittens are most common because the longer days of late winter trigger breeding behavior in unspayed females.
Kitten season, the annual surge in cat births, typically begins in spring and peaks in late spring through early summer.
Animal shelters across North America see a dramatic rise in kitten intakes starting in March each year.
Spaying a cat before her first heat cycle (usually around 5 to 6 months of age) eliminates the risk of certain cancers and prevents unwanted spring litters.
Cats are naturally more active in spring, as warmer weather, more prey, and longer days stimulate their hunting instincts.
Birds returning from migration in spring are a major source of stimulation for indoor cats who watch through windows.
Cats are skilled bird hunters, and one study estimated that domestic cats kill between 1.3 and 4 billion birds annually in the United States.
The arrival of spring insects like moths and flies triggers cats' famous chattering behavior, a sound they make while watching prey.
Many cats are allergic to spring pollen, just like humans, with symptoms including sneezing, runny eyes, and itchy skin.
If your cat sneezes more in spring, it could be seasonal allergies, and a vet can test for specific allergen sensitivities.
Outdoor cats are at higher risk of parasite exposure in spring, when fleas, ticks, and intestinal worms become more active.
Fleas become active when soil temperatures reach above 50 degrees Fahrenheit (10 degrees Celsius), which typically happens in early spring in most climates.
Spring is an important time to start or renew flea, tick, and heartworm prevention for cats that go outside.
Cats instinctively eat grass in spring, which helps them pass hairballs and provides trace minerals and folic acid.
Cat grass (typically wheat, oat, or rye grass) is a safe alternative to outdoor grass that may be treated with pesticides.
Many cats explore outdoors in spring for the first time after winter, so always check that ID tags and microchip information are current.
Spring means more wildlife is active, so keep cats safe from encounters with nesting birds, rabbits, and other animals that may scratch or bite.
A cat's paw pads are sensitive to changes in ground texture in spring, and they often slow-walk across wet grass with exaggerated steps.
Cats will spend hours watching spring rain from a window, as the sounds and movement of water are stimulating to their senses.
Spring cleaning tip: wash all cat bedding, vacuum upholstery, and clean litter boxes more frequently as warm weather increases bacterial growth.
Catnip (Nepeta cataria) blooms in late spring and early summer, and growing your own is easy since cats love interacting with fresh plants.
About 50 to 70% of cats are genetically sensitive to nepetalactone, the compound in catnip that produces a euphoric response.
Cats that don't respond to catnip often respond to silver vine, valerian, or honeysuckle, which are alternative spring botanicals that produce similar reactions.
Spring is a great time to set up a 'catio' (an enclosed outdoor patio for cats) so indoor cats can safely enjoy fresh air and nature.
Butterflies and bumblebees emerging in spring can trigger hours of entertainment and exercise for indoor cats watching through glass.
The smell of fresh earth in spring is stimulating to cats, and many cats become more active and exploratory after spring rain.
Cats' coats look their most glossy and healthy in late spring, after the winter undercoat has shed and the sleeker summer coat grows in.
In the Northern Hemisphere, the spring equinox (around March 20) marks the beginning of the increase in daylight that triggers cats' seasonal shedding.
Cats may gain a slight amount of weight in winter and naturally become more active in spring, so outdoor activities or enrichment can help them slim down.
Keeping a cat's litter box especially clean in warm spring weather helps prevent odors, as higher temperatures accelerate bacterial decomposition.
Spring is the ideal season to introduce leash and harness training for cats who will explore outdoors during warmer months.
Ticks become active in early spring and can transmit diseases including Lyme disease to cats, so check your cat's coat after every outdoor excursion.
The spring transition is a good time to schedule annual vet checkups, vaccinations, and dental cleanings for cats.
Cats love basking in spring sunshine streaming through windows, as sun exposure helps them synthesize vitamin D and regulate their mood.
Cats can get sunburned, especially on light-colored ears, noses, and eyelids, so limit prolonged sun exposure for cats with pale or white fur.
White cats and cats with white ears are at significantly elevated risk of skin cancer (squamous cell carcinoma) from sun exposure.
Spring thunderstorms can frighten cats, so provide them with a safe hiding place and stay calm, as cats pick up on human anxiety.
Many cats enjoy 'spring cleaning' their own territory, spending extra time scent-marking doors, windows, and furniture as the season changes.
Outdoor cats that wander more in spring may bring home more wildlife, so check with your vet about deworming and vaccination updates.
The increase in outdoor activity in spring makes microchipping and collar ID more critical, as cats are more likely to wander far from home.
Spring is when many cat rescues and shelters host adoption events, and if you're ready for a cat, spring is a perfect time to adopt.
Cats experiencing spring shedding benefit from increased hydration, and wet food or water fountains can help reduce hairball formation.
The spring season, with its abundance of sensory stimulation including smells, sounds, textures, and longer playtime, is generally one of the most enriching times of year for a cat.
